1:
The chapter opens with David just past the summit of Olivet, and the witnesses read Ziba's laden donkeys as a study in the danger of a timely gift. John Gill sets the geography — David is a little past the top of the hill he had climbed weeping — while the puritan Matthew Henry supplies the motive that the others only imply: Ziba, content to manage Mephibosheth's estate, now longs to be its master, and reasons that whether David or Absalom prevails he may fish in troubled waters and secure the whole. His present is shrewdly chosen and seasonable, for "a man's gift maketh room for him, and bringeth him before great men" (Prov. 18:16), and "whithersoever it turneth, it prospereth" (Prov. 17:8). Henry draws the sting: if the hope of worldly advantage makes men generous to the rich, shall not the hope of an abundant recompense at the resurrection of the just make us charitable to the poor (Luke 14:14)?

The grammatical-historical commentators fill in the freight and expose the man. Adam Clarke corrects the English "loaves," which suggest our raised bread, to the thin unleavened cakes of the East, and glosses the "summer fruits" as pumpions, cucumbers, or watermelons that refresh travellers in the burning heat, the "bottle" being a goat's skin of wine (compare 1 Sam. 25:18); John Wesley agrees it was a large skin-vessel proportioned to the rest. Charles Ellicott looks ahead to the sequel (2 Sam. 19:24–30) and pronounces the whole story a gross slander: the crippled Mephibosheth could hardly have dreamed that Absalom's revolt would bring him a throne, yet David, hasty in judgment and in no mood to doubt a tale of ingratitude, is deeply moved by so large a contribution to his need. Ellicott finds in Ziba a man of no principle, loyal to David only because his shrewdness foresaw which side would win.

Henry gathers the pastoral lesson the episode presses: Providence graciously sent the food to David's support, for God makes use of bad men for good purposes and, as he fed Elijah, sends his people meat by ravens; yet great men ought always to be jealous of flatterers, remembering that nature gave them two ears that they might hear both sides. Ziba's wine, it is noted, was meant not for the king's own table but as a cordial for those ready to perish (Prov. 31:6) — a mercy of God overruling the malice of man, and the last quiet kindness before the slander of the next verse turns the servant's tongue from feeding the king to poisoning his mind.
2:
David's blunt question — "What meanest thou by these?" — draws from the witnesses a small lesson in Eastern courtesy and a larger one in the providence that feeds a fugitive. John Gill, the reformed voice, hears the king asking whether the beasts and provisions are for sale or a gift, and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch lay out Ziba's tidy inventory: the asses are for the king's household to ride in turn, the bread and summer fruit for the young men to eat, and the wine "that such as be faint in the wilderness may drink" — the same faintness in the desert that had overtaken the company earlier in the flight (compare 2 Sam. 15:23). They note in passing that the Chethib is but a copyist's slip for the plainer reading.

The wesleyan Adam Clarke adds the cultural key that makes Ziba's speech ring true. To present a gift to a great man, he explains, the Easterner speaks with studied self-abasement, as if the offering were fit only for the servants' servants and unworthy of the sovereign himself; the very form of the courtier's words is a compliment. Ziba has learned that art to perfection, and it is precisely his fluency in it that the following verses will unmask.

Beneath the etiquette the witnesses see the same double truth Henry drew from the opening scene. Ziba's motives are self-serving, but the timeliness of the relief is God's, not his: wine for the weak, bread for the weary, beasts for the footsore royal household toiling up the wilderness road. The commentators do not divide here; they read the verse as the smooth opening of a calculated fraud, and yet as a real mercy — the provisions were genuinely needed, and God's providence sent them, whatever the giver intended. The generosity is a mask, but the loaves are bread indeed, and the servant's flattering tongue does not spoil the Lord's kindness to his afflicted king. The scene is quiet, almost domestic, but it sets the stage for the calumny of the next verse, where Ziba turns from provisioning David's body to accusing his absent master.
3:
Now the fraud shows its face. When David asks after "thy master's son," meaning Mephibosheth the grandson of Saul, Ziba answers that his lame master has stayed behind in Jerusalem in the mad hope that "today shall the house of Israel restore me the kingdom of my father." The witnesses are of one mind that this is a lie, and they concentrate on how transparent — and how effective — a lie it is.

John Gill, the reformed voice, identifies the parties: Saul was Ziba's master, Mephibosheth the "son" in view, and the servant's aim is plainly the estate. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch weigh the improbability with care. The "kingdom of my father" makes sense only because the throne would have passed to Jonathan had he outlived Saul; but a cripple lame in both feet, who had never once put forward a claim, could not seriously imagine that a nation which had just crowned Absalom would now turn to him. Ziba is calumniating his master to lay hold of the lands he farms for him; the slander is wildly unlikely, yet in the general confusion of a kingdom in revolt it is not incredible. The wesleyan Adam Clarke is blunter still, branding Ziba a "base wretch" and the charge utterly unfounded against the peaceable, loyal, and innocent Mephibosheth.

The commentators do not quarrel; they combine into a single portrait of malice exploiting a father's grief. What gives the lie its power is the moment: David, freshly betrayed by his own son, is disposed to believe any tale of ingratitude, and Ziba knows it. The very extravagance of the accusation — that a helpless man of Saul's fallen house should dream the whole nation would hand him the crown in a day — ought to have warned the king; instead his haste and his wound make him credulous. The witnesses thus read the verse as a warning against judging the absent by the tongue of the present, and they keep the reader's sympathy for Mephibosheth, whose vindication, they observe, must wait until David returns in peace (2 Sam. 19:24–26).
4:
David's swift verdict — "Behold, thine are all that pertained to Mephibosheth" — and Ziba's oily thanks draw from every witness the same judgement: a rash and unrighteous sentence. They differ only in the shading. John Gill, the reformed voice, grants the legal principle that an estate is forfeited by treason, as Mephibosheth's had once been by the rebellion of Ishbosheth and been graciously restored; had Ziba's charge been true, the confiscation would have been just — but David acts "too hasty," without further inquiry. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch put it more sharply: in his excited state the king "was weak enough to give credence to Ziba's words, and to commit the injustice" of handing the calumniator all that belonged to his victim, for which favour Ziba most politely bows himself to the ground. Charles Ellicott renders the servant's obsequious "I humbly beseech thee" literally — "I bow myself down; let me find favour" — the practised gesture of a man who has just won everything.

The wesleyan commentators press the moral hardest. Adam Clarke calls the conduct "very rash": David "spoiled an honourable man to reward a villain," not giving himself time to examine the case; yet Clarke pleads mitigation — the king was under heavy afflictions, and these "sometimes make even a wise man mad." From it he draws a rule for every reader: nothing should be done rashly, for he who obeys the first impulse of his passions will seldom act rightly and never keep a clear conscience. John Wesley agrees it was "a rash sentence, and unrighteous, to condemn a man unheard, upon the single testimony of his accuser and servant," and hears in Ziba's reply — "thy favour is more to me than this gift" — the flatterer's final, calculated stroke.

There is no real division among the witnesses. Together they hold two things at once: that David sinned by haste, and that grief, not greed, was his undoing. They let the reader feel the pity of it — an innocent cripple stripped in his absence, a good king betrayed into injustice by his own pain — and they quietly note that David would live to be ashamed of this grant when the truth came out and he had to divide the land again (2 Sam. 19:29).
5:
At Bahurim the scene darkens: a man of Saul's house comes out cursing. The grammatical-historical commentators set the ground. Keil and Delitzsch place Bahurim just beyond the Mount of Olives and make Shimei a distant relation of Saul, who comes forth cursing and pelting David and all his servants with stones, though the whole bodyguard marched on the king's right hand and left. Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott add the topography and the politics: the town lay north of Jerusalem in Benjamin — the Almon of Josh. 21:18 and the Alemeth of 1 Chron. 6:60 — and Shimei belonged "to the family, in the larger sense of tribe," of a Benjamin still smarting that the crown had passed from its house; the same grievance would later erupt in Sheba the son of Bichri (2 Sam. 20:1–2). John Gill notes that David was not yet in the town itself but in its fields.

The puritan Matthew Henry supplies the chapter's great contrast, and it governs the whole episode: David "bore Shimei's curses much better than he had borne Ziba's flatteries. By the latter he was brought to pass a wrong judgment on another, by the former to pass a right judgment on himself. The world's smiles are more dangerous than its frowns." Henry reads Shimei's timing shrewdly — he vents his old, hidden hatred now because he thinks it safe, because it will most grieve a wounded man, and because he fancies Providence itself now justifies his reproaches, as Job's friends had argued from his calamity that he must be wicked. "Those that are under the rebukes of a gracious God," Henry warns, "must not think it strange if these bring upon them the reproaches of evil men."

The witnesses do not divide. They agree that Shimei's malice, long nursed while David prospered, seizes the hour of the king's humiliation to trample on one who was down — and they let the base spirit of it stand exposed, insulting over a fallen king whom no generous eye could look on without compassion. Yet even here the commentators point forward: the stones and curses that seem to prove God has forsaken David will become, in his own hands, the occasion of a deeper submission to God, which the following verses unfold.
6:
The stones fly, and the witnesses divide gently over what they signify and what wrong Shimei imagines he is avenging. John Gill, the reformed voice, observes that the stones could not reach the king — the act was contempt more than assault, a declaration that David deserved to be stoned, and (Gill suspects) a pointed allusion to the sin of Bathsheba. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott explains why the missiles missed: the road ran along a narrow ravine, and Shimei kept to the opposite slope, out of reach yet easily heard, close enough to annoy but not to strike — which is why, in the next verse, he must offer to "go over."

Keil and Delitzsch weigh the charge itself. Shimei brands David a "man of drops of blood," crying that Jehovah has now repaid him for "all the blood of the house of Saul, in whose stead thou hast become king," and has given the kingdom into the hand of Absalom his son. What exactly Shimei means by that blood, they confess, "it is impossible to determine with certainty" — perhaps he lays at David's door the deaths of Ishbosheth and Abner, though the narrative has already cleared David of both. The accusation is thus a slander, but a slander with a plausible surface, drawing its force from the ready assumption that a king in flight must be a king under judgement.

The commentators agree on the shape of the scene even where they cannot fill in Shimei's precise grievance: a helpless taunt hurled from a safe distance, stones that fall short, dust that will only blow back into the thrower's own eyes. The puritan Matthew Henry catches it exactly — those who fight against God "cannot hurt him, though they hate him," so that Shimei's malice, which makes him odious, is by its very impotence made ridiculous and contemptible as well. And the publicity of it, flung in the sight of David's mighty men on his right hand and his left, deepens the humiliation the king must swallow, and sets up the servants' angry protest and David's remarkable patience in the verses that follow.
7:
The witnesses turn from the stones to the words, and to the meaning of Shimei's opening cry, "Come out, come out, thou bloody man, and thou man of Belial." John Gill, the reformed voice, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agree that the Hebrew is better rendered "Go out, go out" — a demand that David quit the nation and the kingdom he is charged with usurping, thrown at him with vehemence to hurry his flight. Ellicott doubts whether by "bloody man" Shimei meant more than that he held David responsible for "the blood of the house of Saul" — the deaths of Ishbosheth and Abner and the later execution of Saul's seven descendants at the Gibeonites' demand (2 Sam. 21:1–9) — though he allows that Shimei may also have known of the crime against Uriah and wished to point his curse with that innocent blood.

The puritan Matthew Henry measures the words against the law and the man. To revile the ruler of one's people was a breach of the plain command (Exod. 22:28), and yet what could even a foul mouth find to say against a man so honourable and conscientious as David? Only the old blood of Saul's house — the one thing Shimei himself had lost by. Henry exposes the injustice: no man was more innocent of that blood, for David had twice spared Saul's life while Saul sought his, was many miles away when Saul and his sons fell to the Philistines, and had cleared himself of the murders of Abner and Ishbosheth. "Innocency is no fence against malice and falsehood," he concludes; "nor are we to think it strange if we be charged with that from which we have been most careful to keep ourselves."

The two schools do not contend. Together they read the taunt as a false verdict pronounced upon a wounded king: a demand that he vacate a throne he never stole, and a charge of bloodguilt from which the history itself acquits him. Yet the reader is left to feel the deeper irony the next verses will turn to David's good — that the curse is unjust as Shimei means it, and yet, in another sense David will himself confess, not wholly undeserved.
8:
Shimei now names the theology behind his stones: "The Lord hath returned upon thee all the blood of the house of Saul... and, behold, thou art taken in thy mischief, because thou art a bloody man." The witnesses expose the charge as false in fact and cruel in aim. John Gill, the reformed voice, lists the "false insinuations" folded into it — as if David had secretly stirred up the Philistines against Saul, or had a hand in the deaths of Ishbosheth and Abner — and allows that Shimei may also glance at the seven sons of Saul lately given to the Gibeonites, an event related later though it seems to have happened before (2 Sam. 21:8). John Wesley reads the accusation the same way and hears in "thou art taken in thy mischief" the gloating claim that the same mischief David had brought upon others is now returned upon his own head.

The puritan Matthew Henry lays bare the malice's method. Shimei presses God's own providence into the service of his private revenge, arguing from David's calamity that his cause is lost and his character proved — now that he is dethroned and driven out, the Lord has requited him the blood of the house of Saul. It is the very taunt of the ungodly, "There is no help for him in God" (Ps. 3:2). Henry's caution is sharp: "we must take heed lest we wrong God by making his providence thus to patronise our foolish and unjust resentments; as the wrath of man works not the righteousness of God, so the righteousness of God serves not the wrath of man."

The witnesses stand together. They read the verse as slander dressed in the language of judgement — a man twisting the doctrine of divine retribution into a weapon, and reading a good king's affliction as the proof of secret guilt. Yet they also prepare the turn: the accusation is unjust, and David knows it is unjust in Shimei's mouth; but a humbler charge, which Shimei never named, was even then pressing on the king's own conscience, and it is to that conscience the next verses will bring us.
9:
Abishai's flashing anger — "Why should this dead dog curse my lord the king? let me go over, I pray thee, and take off his head" — gives the witnesses the chapter's sharpest clash between zeal and patience. John Gill, the reformed voice, identifies the speaker as David's nephew, a son of Zeruiah and a general of the army, who "could not bear to hear the king abused in this manner." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch catch the contempt in the epithet: "dead dog," the same phrase of utter worthlessness Mephibosheth had once used of himself (2 Sam. 9:8), and they hear Joab standing with his brother behind the request. Abishai would end the cursing at a stroke, treating Shimei's stone-throwing as the overt act of a man compassing the king's death.

The puritan Matthew Henry reads the moment as a test David passes where lesser men fail. The sons of Zeruiah are "forward to maintain David's honour with their swords," and they resent the affront as well they might; yet the king will by no means suffer it. Henry sets the restraint beside a greater one, recalling how Christ rebuked the disciples who, "in zeal for his honour, would have commanded fire from heaven" upon the village that slighted him (Luke 9:55). To answer a curse with a sword is the instinct of wounded honour; to bear it is the harder and higher thing.

The witnesses do not divide. They present Abishai's fury as natural, even loyal, and not without a certain rough justice — Shimei was indeed breaking the law and endangering the king, and to a soldier's eye his stone-throwing looked like the first stroke of a would-be assassin. But they let David's coming refusal expose the difference between a servant's zeal and a saint's submission. The verse thus stands as the hinge of the episode: the sword is drawn and offered, and the whole weight of the passage now waits on what the humbled king will do with a provocation he has every earthly right to punish. Abishai frames the choice in its starkest form, and it is precisely the ease of the revenge that makes David's restraint so remarkable. That answer, and the threefold reasoning behind it, the next verses supply.
10:
David's reply — "What have I to do with you, ye sons of Zeruiah? so let him curse, because the Lord hath said unto him, Curse David" — is the theological heart of the episode, and the witnesses probe both its submission and its strange ascription of the curse to God. John Gill, the reformed voice, takes the rebuff plainly: the king will neither take his generals' advice nor let them avenge him, for the cursing "will neither hurt me nor you." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch follow the Hebrew closely, noting the words David uses of himself — "perhaps Jehovah will look upon my guilt" — where the older reading is "my iniquity," not the softened Masoretic "mine eye," so that David openly owns a guilt that makes the affliction fitting.

The great care of the witnesses is to keep God's providence over the curse from becoming God's approval of it. Adam Clarke argues on grammar that the particle should be read "for if" rather than "because" — "For if the Lord hath said unto him, Curse David, who shall then say, Wherefore hast thou done so?" — and insists that no one may suppose God ever bade one man curse another; this is the Hebrew idiom that "does not always distinguish between permission and commandment," so that Scripture attributes to God what he only permits. John Wesley makes the same distinction: God "did not put any wickedness into Shimei's heart," for the man's malice was his own; the Lord "said" it "not by the word of his precept, but by the word of his providence," as he is said to command the ravens and to send forth his word to senseless things (1 Kings 17:4; Ps. 147:15). Charles Ellicott gathers the point pastorally: throughout, David "recognises that all his sufferings were from the Lord's hand," and wishes to submit entirely to his will without excusing Shimei's sin.

The debate among them is fine, not fierce — whether the verse is best read "because" or "for if," and how the language of divine command is to be squared with human guilt — but on the substance they are one. The curse is Shimei's crime and David's discipline at once; the same word that damns the reviler sanctifies the sufferer, and David's peace lies in looking past the instrument to the hand of God behind it.
11:
David widens his reasoning: "Behold, my son, which came forth of my bowels, seeketh my life: how much more now may this Benjamite?" The witnesses read it as the argument from the greater trial to the less, and as the deepest confession of the passage. John Gill, the reformed voice, notes that David speaks to quiet Abishai and all his servants, reconciling them to the abuse. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the contrast: the "Benjamite" stands over against David's own son precisely as the partisan of a rival dynasty, and it is telling that David charges Absalom not only with seeking his throne but with seeking his life. John Wesley presses the logic — a son's murderous ambition is a "greater mischief than to reproach me with words," and Shimei is of the very tribe and family "from which God hath taken away the kingdom," so his spite is no wonder; David will not have him hindered by violence, for "it is meet I should bear the indignation of the Lord."

The puritan Matthew Henry distils the lesson: "Tribulation works patience in those that are sanctified. The more we bear, the better able we should be to bear still more." Inured to trouble, David is the less surprised by it — marvel not that enemies are injurious when even friends are unkind, nor that friends are unkind when even children are undutiful.

The most striking voice here is the wesleyan Adam Clarke, who cannot forbear quoting his contemporary Charles Wesley's verse paraphrase of David's mind: "Pure from the blood of Saul in vain, / He dares not to the charge reply: / Uriah's doth the charge maintain, / Uriah's doth against him cry!... Let Shimei curse: the rod he bears / For sins which mercy had forgiven." It is the interpretive key the whole episode has been moving toward. Clarke reads David as taking all this "as being permitted of God for his chastisement and humiliation," acquitted of Saul's blood but arraigned by his own conscience for Uriah's — and so bowing beneath the curse as a just, if bitter, rebuke from heaven. The witnesses close in agreement: the secret of David's patience is not stoic indifference but a penitent's eye, which turns another man's slander into God's own reproof.
12:
David lifts his submission into hope: "It may be that the Lord will look on mine affliction, and that the Lord will requite me good for his cursing this day." Here the witnesses meet a genuine textual crux, and they lay its several readings side by side. John Gill, the reformed voice, gives two: the Lord may "look on mine affliction" with an eye of pity and deliver him, or, following a variant, may "look upon my eye" — the tear of it, as the Targum and the rabbis take it, the weeping wrung from him by his troubles and by Shimei's abuse. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the English follows the Septuagint and Vulgate in "mine affliction," while the Hebrew margin has "mine eye" and the written text "my iniquity" — which he judges "probably the true sense": David hopes God will mercifully look upon the sin he has repented of, and be pleased that its punishment, the curse of Shimei, is patiently borne.

Keil and Delitzsch follow David and his men down the road, with Shimei keeping pace along the opposite hillside, "cursing continually, and pelting with stones over against him, and with earth," until the king comes weary to his resting-place and is refreshed. They read the verse as the quiet centre of the storm — the reviled man walking on, answering nothing, and casting his cause upon God.

The variant readings, far from cancelling one another, deepen the same devotion. Whether God is asked to look on David's misery, on his tears, or on his guilt, the posture is identical: a sufferer who neither retaliates nor despairs, but leaves both the wrong done to him and the wrong done by him in the hand of a righteous God, trusting that the Lord may yet "requite good" for a day of cursing. Ellicott's preferred reading only sharpens the wonder of it — that a man conscious of real guilt should nonetheless dare to hope for good, not because he deserves it but because the God he has wronged is merciful. The witnesses find in the verse the ripest fruit of the whole scene: that faith which can wait, silent under reproach, for a mercy it does not presume but dares to hope, and which reads even the curses of an enemy as possibly the appointed path to blessing.
13:
The narrative slows to a walking pace: "as David and his men went by the way, Shimei went along on the hill's side over against him, and cursed as he went, and threw stones at him, and cast dust." Only two witnesses linger here, and they read the verse as a portrait of dogged malice matched by patient endurance. John Gill, the reformed voice, observes that David kept to the high road toward Bahurim and "took no notice" of the cursing — a forbearance that only emboldened Shimei to grow "bolder and more impudent." Gill marks too the large pause the Hebrew scribes set in the middle of the verse, as if the very text drew breath before the weary king reached his rest.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fix the picture precisely: Shimei walked "on the slope of the hill opposite to him," level with the fugitives across the ravine, cursing without ceasing and flinging both stones and earth "over against" the king. Though the text does not say the missiles struck David, they judge it implied — the whole scene is one of a man venting all the spite he dares from the one safe vantage the ground allowed him.

Set within the larger movement of the chapter, the verse is the outworking of David's resolve in the verses before. Having refused Abishai the sword and confessed the curse to be, in God's hand, a just rebuke, David now simply walks — and Shimei's persistence, meant to break him, becomes instead the arena of his meekness. The commentators do not divide; they let the contrast speak. On the one hillside a man grows louder in his hatred the longer he is not answered; on the road below, a king absorbs stones, dust, and curses in silence, moving steadily toward the shelter the next verse will name. It is a small tableau of the whole biblical pattern — the reviled who, when he was reviled, reviled not again, leaving the outcome with God rather than snatching it back with the sword. Gill's note on the scribal pause is not idle: even the layout of the text seems to hold its breath, as though the sacred writer would have the reader linger over the strange dignity of a king who lets a lesser man rage on unanswered, sure that vindication is God's to give.
14:
The episode ends in exhaustion and relief: "the king, and all the people that were with him, came weary, and refreshed themselves there." The witnesses agree on the mercy of the rest but divide over a single word. John Gill, the reformed voice, reads it as a plain adjective — the king and his people "came weary, with their journey, and through grief and trouble at what they met with" — and finds in the refreshment the first easing after a day of flight and cursing.

The grammatical-historical commentators press a difficulty against that reading. Keil and Delitzsch argue that the sentence "requires that Ayephim should be taken as the name of a place," for otherwise there is nothing to which the following "there" can refer; they note that the resting-place cannot be Bahurim itself, since David's later halt lay well beyond it toward the Jordan (2 Sam. 17:18), and though the name Ayephim is never met again, they take it as a proper name. Charles Ellicott reasons the same way: "the sentence seems to require the mention of some place," and the clause "refreshed themselves there" implies one has already been named, so the word for "weary" is generally read as the proper name Ayephim — "probably a mere caravansary."

The debate is small and friendly, turning wholly on whether a Hebrew word describes the travellers or names their inn; on the substance there is no quarrel. Whether they reached a place called Ayephim or simply "came weary" to their halt, the witnesses see the same thing — a hunted, grieving, reviled company at last set down and given rest. After the flatteries of Ziba, the stones of Shimei, and the long humiliation of the road, the verse closes the flight of David on a note of quiet grace: the weary are refreshed, and the God to whose hand David had committed his cause has begun, in the plainest way, to requite good. The small dispute over a single word is thus a good example of how the witnesses handle the text — pressing the grammar honestly, yet never letting a philological doubt obscure the plain devotional sense the verse yields to every reader. The scene now turns from the fugitive king to the usurper taking his seat in Jerusalem.
15:
The scene shifts to Jerusalem, where "Absalom, and all the people the men of Israel, came... and Ahithophel with him." The witnesses read the usurper's easy entry as a study in the short-lived triumph of the wicked. John Gill, the reformed voice, notes the ominous timing — Absalom reached the city at the very hour David reached Bahurim, barely a mile or so away, so that "had not David made the hasty flight he did, he had fallen into the hands of Absalom." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch mark the arrival of the whole rebel host and the greeting of Hushai the Archite, David's friend, who hails the new king with "Long live the king." Adam Clarke adds a small textual observation, that the words "the men of Israel" are wanting in several ancient versions and manuscripts.

The puritan Matthew Henry supplies the moral frame for all that follows. The gates stood open and none opposed him, so Absalom "came without delay," elated that the design he had feared would be hardest proved so easy. But Henry sees the hand of God in the very smoothness of it: "God suffers wicked men to prosper awhile in their wicked plots, even beyond their expectation, that their disappointment may be the more grievous and disgraceful." Absalom now thinks himself sure of success, for he has the two most celebrated counsellors of the age — Ahithophel, whom he brings with him, and Hushai, who meets him there; "on them he relies, and consults not the ark, though he has that with him."

The witnesses do not divide; they let Henry's verdict govern the chapter's second half. Both of Absalom's oracles, he warns, will prove "miserable counsellors" — the one secretly false to him and the other faithful only to David — so that the usurper's apparent mastery of Jerusalem is already undermined at its foundation. The verse thus opens the counter-movement of the story: David, humbled and praying on the road, has left behind in the city a friend who will turn the rebel's own wisdom against him, and the reader watches a throne founded in sin begin, unseen, to totter.
16:
Hushai steps forward with a double "God save the king," and the witnesses fasten on the craft of the words. John Gill, the reformed voice, notes that David's friend "took the first opportunity to make his court" to Absalom, coming into Jerusalem at the same hour as the usurper. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott restores the literal Hebrew — not "God save the king" but simply "Let the king live" — and reads the scene as a contest of suspicion and stratagem: Absalom is naturally startled, inclined to distrust a man who has just deserted his master, but Hushai will explain his conduct on the principle of loyalty to whatever government stands, urging that it carries divine authority, so that his very faithfulness to the old king becomes a pledge of faithfulness to the new.

Keil and Delitzsch expose the "great craftiness" of the reply. Hushai declares at the outset that Jehovah has chosen Absalom — "whom Jehovah hath chosen, and this people, and all the men of Israel, to him will I belong, and will remain with him" — pretending to read the divine will off the bare fact of Absalom's success. It is a masterpiece of equivocation, true on its face and false in its heart, framed so that every word can be heard by Absalom of himself while Hushai means them of David.

The witnesses do not divide; together they set Hushai's cunning against Ahithophel's coming counsel, the two forming the double hinge on which the chapter turns. They are careful, moreover, not to make Hushai a mere liar: his object is to serve his true king by seeming to serve the false one, and the ambiguity of his speech leaves the deceit, as Clarke will say at the next verse, upon Hushai's own conscience. For now the reader watches the trap being baited — the rebel welcoming into his innermost council the one man most certain to betray him — and remembers Henry's warning that the smiling providence which flatters Absalom is the very net in which he will be taken. The commentators thus balance two judgements throughout: they admire the skill of the ruse as an instrument of God's purpose to defeat Ahithophel, and they leave the honesty of it, as Clarke insists, wholly on Hushai's own head.
17:
Absalom probes the defector: "Is this thy kindness to thy friend? why wentest thou not with thy friend?" The witnesses hear in the taunt both suspicion and self-betrayal. John Gill, the reformed voice, observes that Absalom "would not mention" David's name, nor his title, nor the relation of father, but speaks of him only as "Hushai's friend"; the question is put not in displeasure but to try the newcomer, for Absalom is secretly overjoyed that so trusty a counsellor of David has come over. And Gill turns the reproach back on the speaker: this charge of ingratitude might justly be retorted — is this thy kindness to the father who begot thee and always loved thee, that thou shouldst rebel against him?

John Wesley reads the words the same way, as a test — "doth this action answer that profession of friendship which thou hast hitherto made?" — and hears in Absalom's phrase "thy friend" a sneer "by way of reflection upon David," painting him as a man kind to strangers like Hushai but not to his own son, whom by his severity he had provoked to revolt; and so Absalom "doth not vouchsafe to call him his father." The puritan Matthew Henry notes the deeper folly beneath the cunning: Absalom "easily believes what he wishes to be true," flattering himself that David's best friends are so in love with the usurper that they hasten to declare for him — "the pride of his heart deceived him."

The witnesses do not divide; they read the verse as dramatic irony at Absalom's expense. His question is designed to expose a possible traitor, yet every word of it condemns his own greater treachery: the son who abandoned his father indicts the friend for abandoning his. And his very eagerness to be reassured — to hear that all David's friends will melt into his own party — is the credulity by which Hushai will lead him. The reader sees the snare drawn tighter: the rebel, congratulating himself on a windfall of loyalty, is being played by the one man he should most have feared, and his contempt for the word "father" seals the picture of a heart wholly given over to ambition.
18:
Hushai answers with a sentence perfectly poised between truth and treachery: "Nay; but whom the Lord, and this people, and all the men of Israel, choose, his will I be, and with him will I abide." The witnesses agree that the words are a deliberate equivocation and expose its double edge. John Gill, the reformed voice, simply marks it as the beginning of Hushai's answer to Absalom's questions. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the pledge in full and let it run on into the next verses, where Hushai vows to serve the son as he served the father — the whole speech pitched so that Absalom must hear himself named as the Lord's chosen.

The wesleyan Adam Clarke lays the mechanism bare: "Here is an equivocation. Hushai meant in his heart that God and all the people of Israel had chosen David; but he spake so as to make Absalom believe that he spoke of him." Clarke does not excuse the deceit — "whatever of insincerity may appear in this, Hushai is alone answerable" — and warns that his later words are to be understood in the same double way. The saying is true: the Lord and the people had indeed chosen a king, and Hushai will cleave to him; only the king he means is not the king Absalom hears.

The witnesses do not divide, but they hold the verse at arm's length morally. They neither whitewash Hushai's dissembling nor forget his loyalty; the sin they leave, as Clarke does, to his own account, while noting the sober lesson that God can bring good to David through a stratagem he does not thereby approve. Dramatically, the sentence is the keystone of the deception: Absalom, hearing "the Lord hath chosen," takes it as a benediction on his throne, when in the speaker's mind it is a confession of unshaken allegiance to the fugitive on the Jordan road. The rebel has now formally taken into his cabinet a voice that will, in the very name of piety, work his ruin. And the commentators cannot help observing the deeper irony: the words are truer than Absalom knows, for the Lord had indeed chosen a king and would in the end confirm him — only the chosen one was the father he had driven out, not the son who had seized the throne.
19:
Hushai completes his flattery with a rhetorical question: "And again, whom should I serve? should I not serve in the presence of his son? as I have served in thy father's presence, so will I be in thy presence." The witnesses read it as the second prong of a single, cunning argument. John Gill, the reformed voice, sets out its logic plainly: Hushai offers Absalom "a second reason" for serving him — the first being the supposed choice of God and the people in the verse before, the second being the natural continuity of service passing from father to son, as though the counsellor of David could not more fittingly bestow his loyalty than on David's own child.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch hear the same double meaning that ran through the previous verse: on the surface Hushai binds himself to Absalom as the heir before whom he will stand, yet the words hold true of David, whose son Absalom is, so that the vow of continued service can be secretly kept toward the father even while it is spoken to the son. The wesleyan Adam Clarke, having exposed the equivocation at the previous verse, warns expressly that "what he says afterwards may be understood in the same way" — this appeal to service of "his son" is one more sentence framed to be heard one way and meant another.

The witnesses do not divide. They present the verse as the finishing touch of Hushai's masterpiece of ambiguity: an argument so plausible that Absalom cannot but be flattered, and so worded that Hushai's conscience can still plead he told no outright lie. Beneath the wit the commentators keep the sober note struck at the earlier verses — that a good man's dissembling, however it served the Lord's purpose against the rebellion, is left upon his own account, and that God overrules even a courtier's crooked speech to preserve the king he has anointed. With this the trap is fully set; the scene now turns from the false friend to the fatal counsellor, as Absalom summons Ahithophel to advise what the usurper shall do.
20:
His welcome secured, Absalom turns to the business of seizing the throne in earnest: "Give counsel among you what we shall do." The witnesses read the request as the hinge from taking the capital to keeping the crown. John Gill, the reformed voice, notes that Absalom directs his speech to Ahithophel "as being his first and chief counsellor," the man on whose sagacity the whole enterprise now leans. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe that after taking possession of the capital the next thing required was a plan "to take and keep the throne," and explain the plural "give ye" as addressed to the others present as well, all reckoned capable of advising, though it is Ahithophel whose word will carry.

The puritan Matthew Henry supplies the character of the man consulted, for the verse cannot be read apart from the reputation that made his counsel so weighty. Ahithophel was "cried up as an oracle," his advice reckoned "as if a man had enquired at the oracle of God"; such were his reaches beyond other privy-counsellors that David and Absalom alike revered his judgement — too much by far, Henry adds, when they regarded a mere mortal as an oracle of God. From this he draws his warning: many excel in worldly wisdom who are utterly destitute of heavenly grace, "and the greatest statesmen are seldom the greatest saints"; frequently, too, the ablest politicians act most foolishly for themselves, as Ahithophel did in casting his lot with a rash usurper whose fall any moderate prudence might foresee.

The witnesses do not divide. They present Absalom's appeal as the natural next step of a coup that has so far gone smoothly, and they let Henry's verdict hang over it: the man whose counsel is about to be sought is indeed brilliant, and his very brilliance, unsanctified, will serve a cause founded in sin and therefore founded on the sand. The reader, warned already that both of Absalom's oracles are "miserable counsellors," waits to see what the celebrated Ahithophel will devise — and the next verse delivers advice as shrewd in policy as it is monstrous in wickedness.
21:
Ahithophel's counsel is at once a stroke of cold policy and an abomination: "Go in unto thy father's concubines... and all Israel shall hear that thou art abhorred of thy father: then shall the hands of all that are with thee be strong." The witnesses expose both the shrewdness and the sin. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain the logic: to lie with the king's concubines was to appropriate the royal harem, "a complete usurpation of the throne," an act so irrevocable that it would make reconciliation with David impossible and thereby bind Absalom's followers to his cause with all the greater firmness — which was precisely what Ahithophel hoped to attain. Charles Ellicott agrees that the counsel was meant to make the breach "absolute and irreconcilable," so that the rebels, cut off from all hope of pardon, must persist to the end; and he notes that the seizing of a predecessor's harem was an Oriental custom "to the enormity of which the mind was blunted by the practice of polygamy."

The wesleyan commentators press the horror and the providence together. Adam Clarke recalls the ten concubines left to keep the house (2 Sam. 15:16), and marks that the deed was probably done on the very roof where David's eye had first fallen on Bathsheba (2 Sam. 11:2), so fulfilling the word to Nathan (2 Sam. 12:11); for a son to take his father's wives was "the sum of abomination," death by the law (Lev. 20:11), and he paints Absalom as proud, vindictive, adulterous, and incestuous, yet remembers that "God is as just as he is merciful," for David too had sinned and suffered. John Wesley adds Ahithophel's private motive — revenge for Bathsheba, his own granddaughter, the daughter of Eliam his son (2 Sam. 23:34) — together with the public aim of making the breach "irreparable"; and he reads in the counsel's welcome "the character of Absalom's party, and how abominably wicked they were."

John Gill, the reformed voice, simply notes that Ahithophel, whether of himself or as the council's mouth, gave the advice. The witnesses do not divide: they hold in one view the devilish cleverness of the plan and its unspeakable evil, and above both the righteousness of God, who was even then fulfilling by Absalom's crime the very judgement he had pronounced upon David's.
22:
The counsel is executed in the most public way: "they spread Absalom a tent upon the top of the house; and Absalom went in unto his father's concubines in the sight of all Israel." The witnesses dwell on the terrible fitness of the place. John Gill, the reformed voice, identifies the flat roof of the palace as "the very spot from whence David had a sight of Bathsheba, and conceived an impure lust after her," the Targum calling the structure a canopy of four pillars hung with curtains. John Wesley presses the same point — the deed was done at "the very place from whence David had gazed upon Bathsheba, so that his sin was legible in the very place of his punishment" — and reads Absalom's act as a public claim to the kingdom, since in the East the wives of a late king "belong of right to the successor."

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the theological weight of the scene. Keil and Delitzsch note that the tent was pitched on the roof precisely that the outrage "might be done publicly in the sight of all Israel," and that the counsel behind it was reckoned like a divine oracle. Charles Ellicott draws the exact correspondence Nathan had foretold — that "the nature of David's public punishment should correspond to the character of his secret crime" — and finds it "particularly striking" that the retribution fell on the very roof where David had first yielded to his guilty passion.

The puritan Matthew Henry gathers the meaning the others circle. Absalom, drawing the sword, "did by this provocation throw away the scabbard"; he "declared his sin as Sodom," a tent spread that all Israel might see it. Yet in that very impudence "the word of God was fulfilled in the letter of it," for God had threatened through Nathan that David's wives should be publicly defiled for his defiling of Bathsheba (2 Sam. 12:11). Wesley closes with the same confession: "The Lord is righteous; and no word of his shall fall to the ground." The witnesses do not divide. Whatever they make of the concubines' part or Ahithophel's cunning, they read the roof-top crime as at once the summit of Absalom's wickedness and the exact, appointed harvest of David's own — a solemn proof that the sins men think buried are answered, in God's time and often in God's own dreadful symmetry.
23:
The chapter closes on a backward glance at the counsellor himself: "the counsel of Ahithophel, which he counselled in those days, was as if a man had inquired at the oracle of God: so was all the counsel of Ahithophel both with David and with Absalom." The witnesses read the verse as the narrator's key to all that has happened and all that will. John Gill, the reformed voice, notes that this reputation held "both in the days of David, and in the days of Absalom, before and since the rebellion" — the same oracular weight his word had carried at court now lent to a usurpation. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the Hebrew construction exactly, the whole clause standing in emphatic apposition — "as if one inquired the word of God, so was every counsel of Ahithophel" — and explain that this very estimate is set down to account for why advice with so foul a face as the last should have met with such ready approval.

The wesleyan John Wesley draws out the same purpose: Ahithophel's counsel "was received by the people with equal veneration, and was usually attended with as certain success," and the historian records the fact precisely to explain how a proposal so shameful could win general assent. His authority, not its merits, carried the day.

The witnesses do not divide, and together they let the verse cast a long shadow of irony over the whole narrative. Ahithophel's word was esteemed almost as the very oracle of God — yet it was bent to treason, and the reader who knows the sequel sees the trap in the praise: the man reverenced as heaven's mouthpiece will shortly have his God-like counsel defeated by Hushai's stratagem and, humiliated, take his own life. The commentators thus close the chapter where Henry began it, upon the folly of a wisdom that is not from above: the greatest sagacity of the age, unsanctified, serving a cause founded in sin, and destined for all its brilliance to come to nothing. Human counsel, however oracular in men's eyes, cannot stand when it sets itself against the purpose of the God who overrules even Ahithophel for David's good.
